quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Confidence interval for p"?

options.text: Taking the logarithm of variable values to linearize exponential or power relationships for regression analysis
Compare R² values, residual plots, and prediction accuracy across candidate models to choose the best-fitting transformation
σp̂ = √(p(1-p)/n); decreases as n increases, meaning larger samples give more precise estimates
p̂ ± z*·√(p̂(1-p̂)/n); a range of plausible values for the population proportion based on sample data

quiz_questions.explanation: "Confidence interval for p" is defined as: p̂ ± z*·√(p̂(1-p̂)/n); a range of plausible values for the population proportion based on sample data. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Confidence level"?

options.text: If we repeated the sampling process many times, C% of the resulting intervals would capture the true population mean
t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n); replaces the z-statistic when σ is unknown, using sample standard deviation s in the denominator
The average of the squared deviations from the mean, calculated using n-1 in the denominator for sample data (Bessel's correction)
The long-run proportion of intervals that capture the true parameter; e.g., 95% means 95 out of 100 intervals would contain p

quiz_questions.explanation: "Confidence level" is defined as: The long-run proportion of intervals that capture the true parameter; e.g., 95% means 95 out of 100 intervals would contain p. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Margin of error"?

options.text: With replacement: draws are independent; without replacement: draws are dependent because the population changes after each draw
For Y = a + bX: E(Y) = a + b·E(X) and Var(Y) = b²·Var(X); adding a constant shifts the mean but does not change spread
P(not A) = 1 - P(A); the probability that an event does NOT occur equals one minus the probability it does occur
z*·√(p̂(1-p̂)/n); the maximum likely difference between p̂ and the true p at the given confidence level

quiz_questions.explanation: "Margin of error" is defined as: z*·√(p̂(1-p̂)/n); the maximum likely difference between p̂ and the true p at the given confidence level. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Critical value (z*)"?

options.text: The t-value with area C in the middle of the t-distribution with df = n-1; found from t-table or technology for the desired confidence level
Random sample or random assignment, n < 10% of population, np₀ ≥ 10 and n(1-p₀) ≥ 10
A study in which the researcher observes and records data without imposing a treatment or intervention on the subjects
The z-score corresponding to the desired confidence level: z* = 1.645 for 90%, 1.96 for 95%, 2.576 for 99%

quiz_questions.explanation: "Critical value (z*)" is defined as: The z-score corresponding to the desired confidence level: z* = 1.645 for 90%, 1.96 for 95%, 2.576 for 99%. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Sample size determination"?

options.text: n = (z*/ME)²·p̂(1-p̂); formula for choosing n to achieve a desired margin of error; use p̂ = 0.5 for conservative estimate
The claim we suspect is true and are trying to find evidence for; can be one-sided () or two-sided (≠)
σp̂ = √(p(1-p)/n); decreases as n increases, meaning larger samples give more precise estimates
The distribution of one variable given a specific value of another; found by dividing cell counts by the appropriate row or column total

quiz_questions.explanation: "Sample size determination" is defined as: n = (z*/ME)²·p̂(1-p̂); formula for choosing n to achieve a desired margin of error; use p̂ = 0.5 for conservative estimate. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "p̂ ± z*·√(p̂(1-p̂)/n); a range of plausible values for the population proportion based on sample data"?

options.text: t-distribution
Interpretation of slope CI
Confidence interval for p
Prior probability

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Confidence interval for p".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The long-run proportion of intervals that capture the true parameter; e.g., 95% means 95 out of 100 intervals would contain p"?

options.text: LINE conditions
Confidence level
Chi-square statistic
Confounding variable

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Confidence level".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "z*·√(p̂(1-p̂)/n); the maximum likely difference between p̂ and the true p at the given confidence level"?

options.text: Margin of error
Pooled proportion
Response bias
Association in two-way tables

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Margin of error".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The z-score corresponding to the desired confidence level: z* = 1.645 for 90%, 1.96 for 95%, 2.576 for 99%"?

options.text: Critical value (z*)
Sample proportion (p̂)
Marginal distribution
Homogeneity vs. independence

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Critical value (z*)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "n = (z*/ME)²·p̂(1-p̂); formula for choosing n to achieve a desired margin of error; use p̂ = 0.5 for conservative estimate"?

options.text: Sample size determination
Follow-up analysis
Multiplication rule (general)
Degrees of freedom (df)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Sample size determination".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Confidence Intervals for Proportions is accurate?

options.text: Confidence Intervals for Proportions estimate an unknown population proportion p using the sample proportion p̂ ± z*√(p̂(1-p̂)/n), where z* is the critical value for the desired confidence level.
Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions organize data for two categorical variables and provide the foundation for chi-square analysis.
Paired designs are more powerful than independent two-sample designs because they eliminate subject-to-subject variability, isolating the treatment effect.
For independent random variables: Var(X ± Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y)—variances always add, even for differences.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Confidence Intervals for Proportions?

options.text: Blocking groups experimental units by a known source of variability (such as age, gender, or location) before random assignment within each block, increasing the experiment's ability to detect treatment effects.
The interval gives a range of plausible values for p, with the margin of error z*√(p̂(1-p̂)/n) reflecting both the confidence level and sample size.
Experiments use random assignment to eliminate confounding, allowing causal conclusions.
Paired designs are more powerful than independent two-sample designs because they eliminate subject-to-subject variability, isolating the treatment effect.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Confidence Intervals for Proportions?

options.text: The test statistic χ² = Σ(observed - expected)²/expected measures overall departure from the expected distribution, with larger values indicating worse fit.
Interpretation is crucial: a 95% CI means that 95% of all intervals constructed this way capture the true p.
Linear Regression and Inference extends exploratory regression to formal inference about the population regression line β₀ + β₁x.
Proper identification of variable types determines which statistical methods, graphs, and summary measures are appropriate.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Confidence Intervals for Proportions?

options.text: Interpreting Chi-Square Results requires understanding what a significant χ² value means and what it does not.

The binomial distribution counts the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials with constant success probability: P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1-p)^(n-k), with mean np and standard deviation √(np(1-p)).
Confidence Intervals for Proportions estimate an unknown population proportion p using the sample proportion p̂ ± z*√(p̂(1-p̂)/n), where z* is the critical value for the desired confidence level.

Marginal distributions describe each variable alone (row or column totals divided by the grand total), while conditional distributions show the distribution of one variable within levels of the other.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Confidence Intervals for Proportions?

options.text: Significance Tests for Slopes test H₀: β₁ = 0 (no linear relationship) using t = b₁/SE(b₁) with df = n-2.
This distribution forms the basis for inference about population proportions using z-procedures.
Confidence Intervals for Slopes estimate the true population slope β₁ using b₁ ± t*·SE(b₁) with df = n-2.
The interval gives a range of plausible values for p, with the margin of error z*√(p̂(1-p̂)/n) reflecting both the confidence level and sample size.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Proportions?

options.text: College admissions data examining acceptance rates conditional on gender to investigate potential bias, while being alert for Simpson's Paradox across departments
News organizations reporting poll results with margin of error ('52% favor the measure ± 3 percentage points') derived from the confidence interval formula
Market research testing whether product preference is associated with age group to determine whether different demographics need different marketing strategies
Student performance analysis in a classroom of 25 students using t-distribution (df = 24) rather than z-distribution because the true population standard deviation is unknown

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Confidence Intervals for Proportions?

options.text: Market research firms determining sample sizes needed to estimate the proportion of consumers preferring a product within ±2% with 95% confidence
A/B testing in technology companies randomly assigning users to different website designs to determine which produces higher engagement
Quality control calculating the probability of finding 0, 1, 2, or more defective items in a batch of 50 using the binomial distribution with known defect rate
Medical screening: computing the probability of getting at least one false positive in multiple independent tests using the complement rule: P(at least one) = 1 - P(none)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Confidence Intervals for Proportions applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Public health surveys constructing confidence intervals for vaccination rates in a community to guide immunization campaign decisions
Taste tests where each consumer rates two products, analyzing the paired differences to determine if there is a preference for one product over the other
Evaluating whether a new teaching method causes improved test scores requires an experiment with random assignment—simply comparing schools using different methods is observational
Manufacturing process monitoring using the sampling distribution of x̄ to set control chart limits for detecting when a process mean shifts

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Confidence Intervals for Proportions?

options.text: News organizations reporting poll results with margin of error ('52% favor the measure ± 3 percentage points') derived from the confidence interval formula
Modeling customer arrival times at a store using appropriate probability distributions to optimize staffing schedules
Tobacco litigation using decades of observational evidence (cohort studies, dose-response relationships) to establish smoking as a cause of lung cancer when experiments were unethical
Market research using two-way tables to determine whether product preference varies by age group for targeted advertising strategies

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Confidence Intervals for Proportions in practice?

options.text: Evaluating whether a new teaching method causes improved test scores requires an experiment with random assignment—simply comparing schools using different methods is observational
Market research firms determining sample sizes needed to estimate the proportion of consumers preferring a product within ±2% with 95% confidence
Sociological research examining whether political party affiliation is independent of geographic region using survey data from a national random sample
Social media analysis estimating the proportion of posts containing misinformation and computing a confidence interval using the normal approximation to p̂

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Proportions.
